Battery is excluded not forked (deprecated, removed by Firefox, never in Safari, fingerprinting vector, ~76% Chromium-only) — the fork-existence test in action. Combinator prior-art: Google Chrome Labs react-adaptive-hooks (+ Addy Osmani's adaptive-loading pattern, svelte-adaptive-sensors port) combine exactly hardwareConcurrency+deviceMemory+effectiveType+saveData; WE's capacity provider is the platform-neutral, framework-free version. Every combinator consumes COARSE BUCKETS at the call site (effectiveType '4g', GPU tier 0–3) derived from a raw measurement — which is the new Fork 2 (expose both raw scalar AND derived bucket; default both). Only GPU-tier warrants a maintained dependency: detect-gpu (pmndrs — WebGL benchmark→tier 0–3, handles Apple privacy-masked renderer strings); CPU/RAM/network are thin native reads, battery excluded — delegation met with ONE lib. Recommended: Fork 1 separate CapacityProvider [high], Fork 2 both scalar+bucket [med-high], Fork 3 CompositeProvider routing by check-domain [high]. Full grounding in reports/2026-06-15-device-capacity-provider.md.

title: Embedded-demo overlay escape — how an iframed demo's modal covers the host page
summary: Prep research for decision #732 (a carved fork of the embed-mechanism epic #728). The fuiDemo shortcode (.eleventy.js:38) embeds a FUI-hosted demo in a sandboxed, fixed-height iframe (sandbox='allow-scripts allow-same-origin', style='height:${h}px'; .fui-demo wrapper has overflow:hidden, style.css:1657). A demo's modal/popover/toast can't escape that box to cover the host docs page. The load-bearing finding is a PLATFORM CONSTRAINT, not an effort tradeoff: the top layer (<dialog> showModal / ::backdrop, the Popover API) is PER-DOCUMENT, so an iframe document's top layer is confined to the iframe's box — by security design, no iframe content can paint outside its frame. This is exactly the crux of the WE Dialog block (blocks.json:3286), whose thesis is 'native-first top-layer mount via <dialog> showModal + ::backdrop, never a position:fixed re-implementation' — so the Dialog family (modal/drawer/sheet) is the first #604 block that visibly breaks inside fuiDemo, the concrete trigger #732 asks for. Prior art reshaped the forks: the resize concern (auto-size the frame to content) is a SOLVED, de-facto-standard protocol — iframe-resizer (davidjbradshaw, v5; child posts content size via postMessage, parent resizes, MutationObserver/ResizeObserver-driven, child-initiated to bypass cross-origin) and oEmbed's real-world height-postMessage pattern (Twitter/Instagram ignore the spec's fixed height and post height instead) — so WE should REUSE that message shape, never mint a WE resize protocol (minimize-lock-in). The overlay-escape concern splits into TWO host-side flavors the original item collapsed into one 'B': B1 — host PROMOTES the existing frame to a fixed full-viewport layer (the Intercom messenger model: host toggles position:fixed;inset:0 on the frame; the demo paints its own backdrop, now covering everything; one boolean open/close message) vs B2 — host renders the backdrop/positioning NATIVELY (demo posts overlay intent+geometry; host paints its own <dialog>/popover, syncs geometry + focus-trap handoff). Net classification: auto-resize rides along as a baseline (strictly better than fixed pixel heights across themes/viewports) once any channel exists; origin-validated postMessage is a forced invariant (the payment-skimmer overlay/postMessage-spoof attack surface, per The Hacker News 2025); the overlay message contract stays shortcode-local/unregistered until a SECOND embed consumer appears (per #728's own slicing ruling — component.njk:235 is the only consumer today), then registers as a webembed-style protocol. The genuine call is the escape mechanism: recommended A-contained baseline + B1-promote on opt-in [med — B1 vs B2 is where judgment lives], B2 rejected as over-built for a 'see it run' demo, C (defer to the #700-gated DI mount) unavailable today. Full grounding in reports/2026-06-16-iframe-overlay-escape.md.

title: WE↔FUI in-document mount — relaxing the isolation boundary for the trusted pair
summary: Prep research for decision #765 (carved from the overlay-escape ruling #732, child of the embed epic #728). The constellation states an ABSOLUTE docs-rendering boundary — 'WE never imports or renders FUI block code in its own document' (docs/agent/demo-workflow.md:31); WE surfaces a FUI block only by embedding its FUI-hosted demo through the sandboxed fuiDemo iframe (.eleventy.js:38). #732 ruled overlay escape is solved host-side over the iframe (modes A/B1/B2 of a FUI-owned embed SDK) and recorded a fourth mode — C, an in-document/DI mount that drops the iframe and mounts the FUI component directly in WE's host DOM — as 'no longer never: a future trust-gated option for the WE↔FUI pair only', deferred to #765. The DECISIVE finding is iframe-impossible fidelity: a <dialog> (or popover) INSIDE A SHADOW ROOT can showModal()/showPopover() and promote to the HOST document's top layer, painting above all page content — true even for a closed shadow root (Open UI Popover explainer); the top layer ignores DOM position, makes the page inert, ::backdrop covers the viewport (MDN Popover API). An iframe structurally cannot (its top layer is its own document's — the #732 constraint). So C gets NATIVE, page-covering overlays with ZERO coordination, where A/B1/B2 simulate escape host-side via postMessage. Shadow DOM scopes style+DOM but is NOT a JS/security sandbox (one shared global scope; closed is 'not a strong security mechanism'; inheritable props + custom properties pierce in BY DESIGN — the theming channel) — structural encapsulation, not a trust boundary, which is exactly why it fits a TRUSTED pair. Every in-document composition model (single-spa, Module Federation, Web Components) mounts into one shared runtime presuming same-org trust; embed vendors iframe the UNTRUSTED surface (Stripe card fields for origin isolation) and script the trusted orchestration ('isolation buys safety, tight integration buys UX', Appmixer). Iframe isolation exists FOR cross-org untrusted content (arxiv) — so WE's 'never' is a cross-organization rule, and WE↔FUI is one constellation/same author/complete trust, making the exception principled not a hole. Residual risk the 'keep absolute' branch weighs: inheritable-CSS bleed (by design), shared globals + duplicate runtime, one-shot customElements.define tag collisions, first-party supply-chain surface CSP can't sandbox — against which C buys FIDELITY ONLY (A/B1/B2 already cover escape). Classification: #765 is an architecture/governance ruling spawning no entity; the mount is impl→FUI (a render mode of the embed SDK, runtime bundle not the #700 source import). Fork-existence test: both branches coherent, but the constellation states a POLICY/INVARIANT — 'never' is either absolute or carries the C exception, can't be both — so it's a genuine either/or, ONE real fork. Recommended RELAX, narrowly gated: sanction C as a future trust-gated, WE↔FUI-only, runtime-SDK, Shadow-DOM-isolated, opt-in (iframe-default) render mode, impl FUI-owned [med-high — punctures a stated absolute invariant for a fidelity-only gain]; guard conditions are forced invariants, light-DOM the broken sub-branch (Shadow DOM forced). Full grounding in reports/2026-06-16-we-fui-in-document-mount.md.

title: Parcel trait Enforcer adapter — config delivery to a declarative (named) plugin
summary: Prep research for decision #756 (5th-bundler child of the trait-tree-shaking epic #715). The four shipped trait Enforcer adapters share one factory shape — traitEnforcerX(options) taking a JS traitMap — and one core, buildTraitManifestSource (vite-plugin.ts:274-282), wrapped per bundler (Vite/Rollup load hook, esbuild contents, webpack data: URI). #744 carved Parcel out because its plugin model is DECLARATIVE: a Resolver is referenced BY NAME in .parcelrc and instantiated by Parcel, so (the reasoning went) the Map can't be a JS factory argument. The survey confirms the mechanism is sound — a Parcel Resolver's resolve() returns a ResolveResult whose `code` field IS a virtual module ('used rather than reading the file from disk'), so the adapter returns { filePath: virtualId, code: buildTraitManifestSource(opts) }, reusing the core unchanged; resolvers run as a null-passes-on pipeline. A named .parcelrc plugin carries NO inline options and reads config the idiomatic way via loadConfig→getConfig([files],{packageKey}) — searches up the tree for JSON5/JS/TOML files + a package.json key (Sass: getConfig(['.sassrc','.sassrc.js'],{packageKey:'sass'})). The LOAD-BEARING finding overturns #744's premise: since Parcel v2.9.0, plugins may be referenced as a RELATIVE PATH to a local JS file (no package.json needed), so a project can author a tiny local resolver that imports a WE factory and passes the Map as a JS arg captured in a closure — PRESERVING the cross-bundler traitEnforcerX(options) symmetry with no new config format and no published package. So the genuine fork is not 'the Map can't be a JS arg' but declarative-config vs local-wrapper. Reshaped to ONE real call (Map delivery: A idiomatic .trait-enforcerrc/packageKey via loadConfig — most native-Parcel, cache-correct, but mints a project-facing format and breaks factory symmetry; B local wrapper preserving the factory via v2.9.0 relative-path — recommended, keeps symmetry, no new format, consistent with how all four adapters are consumed, invalidateOnStartup covers the static Map; C WE-generated config — rejected, minimize-lock-in) plus two forced invariants (virtual-module-via-{filePath,code}; hand-authored+documented .parcelrc registration). Dep scope: real build needs the full @parcel/config-default stack + @parcel/plugin, not just @parcel/core (#744's assumption corrected). Recommended B [med-high]. Full grounding in reports/2026-06-16-parcel-trait-enforcer-config-delivery.md.

title: Rendered-site regression tooling — in-repo specs vs a hosted service (a11y · content · visual)
summary: Prep research for decision #799 (child of epic #800): where rendered-docs-site regression checks live — Playwright specs in the WE repo vs a unified harness homed in plateau-app as a service WE CI consumes. The standing test dissolves the binary: per the #091 Web-Docs-as-a-Service ruling, a managed offering is not a monolith needing a home — it decomposes across the constellation, and the 2025–2026 prior art shows exactly one universal seam that confirms the same split: CI owns the pass/fail GATE; a hosted service owns only baseline STORAGE + the diff-REVIEW UI + historical dashboards. Visual regression is the one category that trips the three conditions for needing that service — (a) baselines are large binaries needing branch-aware promotion, (b) pixels are environment-sensitive so a centralized render runner is needed (Argos: 'screenshots from CI, not laptops'), (c) a diff is a human-judgment approval. Hosted SaaS: Chromatic, Percy, Applitools, Argos (also OSS/self-hostable). BYO-storage: reg-suit (your S3 + GitHub PR comment). Fully in-repo: BackstopJS, Playwright toHaveScreenshot (baselines in git). a11y (axe-core/playwright, Pa11y, Lighthouse CI) needs NO baseline or service — deterministic pass/fail in-repo; Lighthouse CI is the textbook split (`@lhci/cli` assert gates with no server; the optional `@lhci/server` adds ONLY historical trends, off the critical path). WE's shipped #770 a11y gate already embodies this (axe in the Playwright lane, no storage). Content/data-binding correctness has no SaaS category — the pattern is Playwright assertions comparing extracted DOM text to the PROJECTION of the source data (Testing-Library 'test what the user sees' + Pact consumer-driven-contract analogue), never hard-coded fixtures (#796 already mandates this). Classification against the constellation: contract (route allowlist tests/a11y/route-allowlist.ts, loader-projection extraction) → WE; portable harness primitives (runner glue, axe wrapper, projection-extraction utils) → FUI open primitive (extracted only when a 2nd consumer / the visual slice forces it — timing is prioritization, a separate build, not a fork); served surface (visual baseline store + review UI + dashboard) → plateau-app product, open-core. So a11y (#770) + content (#796) ship NOW as WE-repo Playwright specs (their layer is WE-resident, deterministic, no service); the FUI/plateau layers materialize when the visual slice forces them. Two residual forks: (1) who owns the gate signal — A WE CI invokes the harness and owns red/green [recommended, matches the seam + the user's 'a service the WE CI calls' phrasing] vs B plateau runs checks against deployed WE-docs and reports back [rejected as the gate model, couples WE's build gate to a deployed service]; (2) the deferred visual slice — A owned plateau-app visual-regression service [recommended per minimize-lock-in + linear-cost-with-revenue + #091] vs B third-party SaaS [rejected: lock-in + per-snapshot cost] vs C committed baselines [interim bootstrap only]. Full grounding in reports/2026-06-16-rendered-site-regression-tooling-home.md.

title: FUI catalog-block-family denominator + dir→WE-spec mapping rule
summary: Prep research for decision #783 (slice b of the FUI block-catalog completeness epic #731, executing the #706 ruling). #706 ratified a fixed invariant — every implemented frontierui/blocks/ family must resolve to ≥1 manifest entry, gated like WE's check-demos forces every demo folder to be registered — but left the load-bearing word FAMILY undefined: the gate's DENOMINATOR (which blocks/ dirs are catalog families vs infra), the dir→WE-spec MAPPING rule, and what to do with no-spec dirs. Verified the tree: blocks/ has 24 dirs; FUI's impl manifest (src/_data/blocks.json, 23 entries, filled by the resolved #737) already carries a sourcePath on every entry pointing at the dir it realizes (blocks.json:10 'blocks/stores/simple'). With renderers/ (index.ts:11 re-exports the published @frontierui/jsx-runtime #265 + data-grid-internal helpers), traits/ (FUI trait impls governed by the SEPARATE traits.json gate, check-standards.mjs:95-116), and __tests__/ set aside, all 21 remaining top-level dirs already map — so a top-level-dir denominator is GREEN TODAY with zero new entries; the disease #706 named (16 whole families unpublished) is cured by #737, the gate keeps it cured. Most of #783 is NOT a decision once classified: (1) MAPPING = sourcePath-anchored, not name-matched — forced invariant handling name-mismatch (stores→simple-store, navigation→nav-list, transient→transient-component, text-nodes→interpolation-text-node, audit→audit-trail) and multi-block (parsers→3 specs) BY CONSTRUCTION; a dir-name==id rule is broken; (2) INFRA exclude-set {__tests__, traits, renderers} — forced (tests; double-governed traits; a published-package re-export); (3) no-WE-spec DIRS are exactly the three infra dirs → exclude. PRIOR ART (CEM analyzer, Storybook, Nx) converges: discovery keys off an explicit MARKER with the unit's name DECOUPLED from its directory path (CEM resolves tag names from customElements.define(), never dirname; Storybook globs *.stories.* files; Nx keys off project.json marker files), infra excluded by absence-of-marker not a denylist — strongly confirming the sourcePath mapping invariant and steelmanning the registration-name granularity branch. The ONE genuine on-merit fork: denominator GRANULARITY — A flat top-level dir + explicit infra exclude-set (recommended: mirrors check-demos which #706 cited, pure-filesystem/implementer-agnostic, deterministic, green today; blind to same-dir sibling drift like nav:section) vs B registered-name (catches nav:section, but couples the WE-standard-owned invariant to FUI's registration convention + reintroduces an internal-exclusion list for the call/pipe/value sub-parsers). Recommend A [med-high]. Rejected sub-option: leaf-dir recursion (reintroduces the internal-exclusion problem AND still misses same-dir siblings). Captured follow-up (not a fork branch): nav:section (NavSectionBehavior, a registered W3C-APG Disclosure Navigation behavior) has no WE spec — file a build to author it, independent of the gate. Layer: a build-time devtools/check, no runtime/DI/Protocol; the invariant is WE-standard-owned, the denominator code is FUI's instantiation (#784). Full grounding in reports/2026-06-16-fui-catalog-family-denominator.md.

title: Per-component token-table data sourcing for the Web Docs /blocks/ panels
summary: Decision prep for #802 (one of three panel-data calls split from #727, under the Web Docs feature-pipeline epic #623). A per-component /blocks/{id}/ page should show a token table; #727 traced the build premise false and carved this to its own decision: where the per-component token data comes from, how a block maps to a component-token group, and what the table shows. NOT greenfield — the token model (#403/#404) and CEM protocol (#653/#626 Fork 1) both shipped. Verified the tree: webtheme/defaultTokens.ts:90-117 has a component tier but only button/card (2 of 69 blocks), each aliasing a primitive, and those keys are NOT block ids (closest is action-button; there is no card block) — it names generic archetypes, not WE blocks. webtheme/tokens.ts:78-140 already exposes flattenTokens()/resolveTokens() yielding each token alias target + resolved literal (the three columns a table wants); compile.ts emits the #403 example --button-radius: var(--radius-md). The pivotal finding: the CEM WE already emits (custom-elements.json via scripts/gen-cem.mjs, #653) has a first-class per-component cssProperties slot the props-table already claims to render (block-descriptions/props-table.njk:17), but gen-cem.mjs:71-82 emits NONE today and 0 blocks carry token data — the renderer slot exists, wired into the same manifest the props table consumes, simply unpopulated. PRIOR ART converges: Material 3 (ref/sys/comp tiers; component tokens alias a system token and the docs show the alias chain), Adobe Spectrum (component tokens reference alias/global tiers), the CEM cssProperties slot (de-facto multi-vendor home — api-viewer/Storybook autodocs/VS Code custom-data — the same one-manifest-many-consumers principle #626 Fork 1 ratified for the props table), and Storybook design-token addons + Style Dictionary (both key off an explicit marker, never dir/name convention, and treat the token REFERENCE as first-class with the resolved value alongside). THREE prepared forks: (1) DATA SOURCE — recommend CEM cssProperties projected by gen-cem.mjs over a parallel src/_data token feed (applies the one-manifest ruling to tokens); coherent-but-excluded alternative = a standalone token JSON. (2) block-to-token MAPPING locus — recommend an explicit optional componentTokens field on the blocks.json entry (mirrors the #727 fuiDemo precedent, keeps webtheme/ pure), broken branch = id name-convention (button != action-button). (3) table SCOPE — recommend override + alias + resolved literal (M3/Spectrum-aligned superset; data already in ResolvedToken). Red-team flags: Fork 1 default leans on #626 Fork 1 being load-bearing (the parallel-JSON branch is functional, only excluded by the one-manifest principle); sparse 2/69 coverage is a PRIORITIZATION concern (when to build), not the sourcing decision. Layer: build-time docs projection riding the existing DTCG/CEM protocols, no new protocol/intent/DI. Full grounding in reports/2026-06-16-component-token-table-sourcing.md.

title: Per-component API data sourcing for the Web Docs props table (authored vs FUI CEM analyzer)
summary: Decision prep for #801 (slice of Web Docs feature pipeline epic #623). The props-table renderer (block:props-table #654) and the CEM protocol + emit pipeline (scripts/gen-cem.mjs #653) already ship, but gen-cem.mjs:43-93 projects only events/exports/tagName and custom-elements.json carries 0 attributes/slots/cssProperties for all 74 blocks — so the table has no data for four of its six member kinds. #801 frames the call as a boundary fork: A hand-authored blocks.json fields (WE-side declared contract, projected by gen-cem) vs B an FUI-side CE-manifest analyzer over FUI block source shipped to WE as data. GOVERNING PRECEDENT: #706 (resolved) already dissolved the same authored-vs-impl-scan question into invariant+dimension — derivation-source is a SUPPORTED DIMENSION (authored | impl-scan | hybrid, all emitting the same CEM contract) with WE shipping authored->CEM (#626/gen-cem) as the default/reference source, impl-scan opt-in (#785 documented it). So #801 is the application of that ruling to WE’s own props-table; the default is already named = authored. BOUNDARY: for WE’s own reference docs, B (an FUI build artifact consumed as WE’s canonical SoT) is EXCLUDED by the docs-rendering boundary (@webeverything never consumes FUI artifacts as canonical; WE renders its own standard pages) and inverts contract-first (WE declares, FUI implements). WE already authors the public contract WE-side: blocks.json declares events (type-ahead’s 3 events w/ detail/taxonomy) that gen-cem projects — attributes/slots/cssProperties/cssParts are the same nature. DECISIVE PRIOR ART: the CEM analyzer CANNOT auto-derive slots, CSS custom-properties, or CSS parts — those are hand-authored as JSDoc in source regardless (analyzer getting-started: it figures out most of the API but needs JSDoc help for CSS Shadow Parts, CSS Custom Properties and Slots; Shoelace/Lit manually add @slot/@csspart/@cssproperty and the analyzer scrapes them). So four of the six props-table columns are hand-authored either way — B’s headline advantage (zero authoring, single impl SoT) is false for exactly the fields the table needs; the only question is which repo’s file the authored contract lives in, and the boundary answers WE-side. The only class the analyzer genuinely automates is the programmatic JS surface (non-reflected fields/methods) — the impl-volatile, FUI-owned surface. After the fork-existence test: TWO FORCED INVARIANTS — I1 source = authored WE-side blocks.json -> gen-cem -> CEM (alt broken for WE docs); I2 field shapes mirror CEM 2.1.0 member kinds, no bespoke schema (#654 consumesCemNotBespoke). The FUI analyzer survives as the #706 opt-in impl-scan dimension + the engine of a conformance gate (analyzer CEM checked vs WE-declared CEM, failing on drift — #463 posture). ONE GENUINE ON-MERIT FORK: scope of the authored contract — A full CEM member set incl. programmatic JS members/methods (pulls FUI’s impl-volatile surface into the WE standard) vs B platform-facing contract surface only (attributes, reflected properties, events, slots, cssProperties, cssParts; defer non-reflected JS members/methods to the #706 opt-in impl-scan). Recommend B [med-high] — the platform-facing surface IS the standard’s contract and must be authored regardless; the programmatic surface is FUI’s and the only class an analyzer adds value for. Captured follow-ups (builds, not forks): extend gen-cem + props-table page-integration; backfill authored member fields; conformance gate. Layer: no new runtime/DI/Protocol/Block/Intent — authored data on the existing custom-elements-manifest protocol (#653), projected by the existing gen-cem devtool. Full grounding in reports/2026-06-16-per-component-api-data-sourcing.md.

title: Per-component a11y-panel content + data sourcing for the Web Docs /blocks/ pages
summary: Decision prep for #803 (the third of three panel-data sourcing calls split from #727, under the Web Docs feature-pipeline epic #623; siblings #801 props-table API and #802 token table). Decide what a per-component accessibility panel on /blocks/{id}/ surfaces and where its data comes from. Verified the tree: authored a11y/standards metadata ALREADY exists and is UNRENDERED — src/_data/blocks.json carries an optional webStandards field on 28 of 74 blocks (first at blocks.json:25), shaped {<concern>: {usage, reference}} (e.g. wizard.webStandards.ariaCurrentStep = aria-current usage + an MDN link); across all entries the reference field points at the CONTRACT (95 MDN + 12 W3C/WAI incl. APG + a few Open-UI/WHATWG), and src/block-pages.njk renders NONE of it (grep webStandards -> 0). intents.json (56 intents) has NO a11y/aria/wcag field, so an intent-derived contract is a NEW schema, not a projection. #770 (resolved) ships a ROUTE-level axe gate (tests/a11y/rendered-site-a11y.spec.ts + route-allowlist.ts) whose GATED_ROUTES include the /blocks/ index but NOT /blocks/{id}/, and store no per-component metadata. The block the panel documents renders inside a cross-origin FUI iframe (the fuiDemo shortcode, .eleventy.js:38, sandbox=allow-scripts allow-same-origin, FUI-hosted) — per #732 axe run from the WE lane CANNOT traverse into the cross-origin frame, and FUI owns the impl AND its rendered display (the no-leakage boundary, #701/#700/#732). PRIOR ART (design-first step 1): WAI-ARIA APG = the canonical implementation-agnostic CONTRACT (keyboard-interaction + roles/states/properties); IBM Carbon = the only surveyed system that visibly SEPARATES three layers (authored 'Accessibility considerations' + a 'Resources' link to the APG pattern + dated AVT automated/keyboard/screen-reader test status + a per-component status matrix); Storybook addon-a11y = runtime-only live axe-core, NOT persisted, a dev-time linter (docs: axe covers up to 57% of WCAG); Material 3 / Fluent 2 / GOV.UK = hand-authored prose with no verification surfaced; Adobe React Aria = authored implementation-REALIZATION prose. SYNTHESIS: the item's three 'options' (intent-derived / per-demo axe / authored notes) are a pass-0 miss — they are not competing alternatives but the THREE LAYERS (contract -> intent, realization -> block webStandards, verification -> conformance/FUI), and Carbon keeps all three SEPARATE; merging them implies a sufficiency claim axe can't back (treat automated results as a separate DATED attestation, never authored prose, never proof). THREE prepared forks: (1) realization SoT — recommend extend & render the existing block webStandards field (already on 28/74, impl-specific = exactly what a per-component panel shows) over minting a new a11y schema or parallel feed; (2) contract layer — recommend LINK OUT via each concern's existing reference (Carbon 'Resources' model) and DEFER a structured intent-layer a11y contract to its own item (triggered by a 2nd consumer), vs minting intent a11y now; (3) verification — recommend NONE in v1 (route-level #770 stands; a future per-component signal arrives only as FUI-published no-leakage output, #475), broken branch = a per-block axe lane on /blocks/{id}/ (can't traverse the cross-origin fuiDemo iframe; audits WE chrome, not the component). Panel shape: an 'Accessibility & Web Standards' section = one row per webStandards concern -> usage prose + a 'Reference' link to the contract. Red-team flags: Fork 1 leans on webStandards being a REALIZATION record (spot-check the usage prose is impl-specific, not pattern-generic); Fork 3 leans on the #732 cross-origin finding + #475 no-leakage still governing (if FUI already publishes per-component axe output, the 'none in v1' default weakens). Layer: build-time docs render of an existing authored block field; no new protocol/intent/DI. Full grounding in reports/2026-06-16-component-a11y-panel-sourcing.md.

title: Design-System Creator/Assembler Open-Core Tiering
summary: Open-core tiering prior-art survey grounding #775 — where the free/paid line sits for the design-system creator that emits a #747 manifest, after #091 settled the two-tier split (simple FUI-native free assembler + full Plateau creator #751). Surveyed how comparable theme/token/design-system tools tier: Tokens Studio (closest analog — free = manual authoring + edit + git sync + export; Pro = connect-to-Figma-Styles, multi-file sync, branch switcher, composition tokens, collaboration), Material Theme Builder (free, including DTCG/DSP import+export + Style Dictionary codegen), Style Dictionary (free OSS DTCG transform), Codia/AI screenshot→tokens (~$49/image, per-call metered), and the DS platforms Supernova/zeroheight/Knapsack (free starter; $20-$399/mo for hosted persistence + collaboration + governance + seats). The line is tri-partite and universal: (1) manual deterministic locally-runnable authoring → FREE; (2) per-call variable-cost automation (AI/vision) → PAID, metered; (3) managed integration + hosted persistence + collaboration → PAID. Inside import the split is open-standard format (DTCG — free everywhere) vs proprietary-vendor managed integration (Figma-Styles — the canonical Pro feature). Mapped onto WE's rulings the line is almost entirely FORCED: the two-tier split by #091 (self-host floor + open-core by usage), manual authoring free by the self-host floor (the #747 manifest is a WE standard artifact), vision PAID by #475 (no-leakage Plateau service) + the linear-cost rule, persist/share/collab PAID by #751's sign-in upsell. The only genuinely-open calls: Fork 1 the partitioning PRINCIPLE (recommend the capability-cost line: free = deterministic/fixed-cost/locally-runnable producing a self-hostable manifest; paid = per-call cost OR hosted/collaboration/managed-integration; rejected = manual-only-free and quota-gating the floor) and Fork 2 the one knob the principle most contests, deterministic format import (recommend DTCG import free, proprietary Figma ingest paid as a managed Plateau integration). Full grounding in reports/2026-06-16-design-system-creator-open-core-tiering.md.

title: Runtime Block Composition Across the WE↔FUI Boundary
summary: Prior-art survey grounding #812 (the consumption-path decision that gates the #697 blocks-deletion cutover): can a UI implementation (FUI) be consumed by a separately-owned host (WE) at RUNTIME as composable component classes/factories — not just pre-rendered embedded widgets — across a trust boundary, without that collapsing into a runtime import? Surveyed Webpack 5 Module Federation, Angular Native Federation, import-maps/ESM-CDN, web-components-as-federation-unit, the Geers/Fowler micro-frontend taxonomy, and real cross-trust SDKs (Stripe Elements, Intercom). Decisive finding (high confidence): the dividing line across every pattern is class vs. element/mount — boundary-PRESERVING runtime patterns hand the host an opaque unit (iframe / custom element / render-function signature) and the host composes around it; boundary-COLLAPSING patterns hand over the class/module (Module Federation, ESM-CDN), which is a runtime import by another name with identical trust blast radius (the code runs in the host's realm with the host's privileges, sharing its dep graph). So #812's option (c) — 'extend #765's SDK to hand the app composable FUI block classes at runtime' — is struck: it is the #707-forbidden import with extra ceremony, not a principled third path. #765's in-document mount of a PRE-RENDERED component behind a shadow root is the correct, prior-art-aligned managed-mount shape and structurally cannot give an app composable classes — so the exercise apps that need classes to wire behaviors must live in FUI (#812 Fork 1, option a), and the loader→background handoff demo decouples onto the escalation contract (#812 Fork 2, option d). Full grounding in reports/2026-06-16-812-runtime-block-composition-boundary.md.

title: Custom-element tagName naming convention — what value WE specifies for the #822 WE-owned contract
summary: Prep research for decision #841 (gating the tagName-value half of the #822 build, under Block Explorer epic #746). #822 ruled the custom-element surface is a WE-owned CONTRACT (FUI conforms); #841 decides what tagName VALUE WE specifies. The survey of the real tree RESHAPED the framing. Findings: (1) blocks.json carries ZERO tagName today and gen-cem.mjs NEVER fabricates a tag (scripts/gen-cem.mjs:68-80). (2) Element-ness is sparse — of 75 blocks, only 5 custom ELEMENTS register in FUI via customElements.define (auto-heading, auto-complete, route-view, route-outlet, background-tasks); most type:Component blocks register as behaviors/attributes/functions (tabs/stepper/tree-select/master-detail), so a tag CANNOT be inferred from type:Component without fabricating fictional elements. (3) A block is NOT 1:1 with a tag — router registers TWO elements (route-view + route-outlet); autocomplete→auto-complete and transient-component→auto-heading show tag≠id, so a pure prefix+id derivation is structurally impossible and the value must be overridable. (4) FUI's 5 tags are bare kebab with NO prefix; there is no fui- prefix anywhere, and fui-<id> would brand the WE-owned contract with the impl name (category error). (5) 24 single-word ids (dropdown/menu/dialog/…) are INVALID as bare custom-element tags (WHATWG requires a hyphen), so a uniform prefix mechanically guarantees validity catalog-wide. Prior art: WHATWG mandates only name SHAPE (hyphen-required, reserved-name denylist), no prefix/id-map policy; the component-library ecosystem strongly converges on one short vendor/namespace prefix per library (sl-, md-, sp-, fluent-, ion-, lightning-/c-) to guarantee the hyphen and avoid global-registry collisions; CEM resolves the tag from the define() call string, never from filename, and models a module emitting MANY declarations (matching router→2 elements); scoped custom-element registries are the platform analog of #822 deep-JSX-dissolves-the-global-tag. This splits the original single fork into: Fork 1 — element-ness is OPT-IN/authored (default), since auto-derive-from-Component is BROKEN (fabrication); Fork 2 — value-default posture: regular derivable convention <prefix>-<id> with FUI 5 names migrating to conform (default, ~75%) vs per-entry authored ratifying FUI existing names; plus a Supported-by-default configurable prefix (platform default we-, design-system projects #746 override; fui- excluded). The convention binds ONLY global-registration modes; compile-time/JSX + DI-override stay totally flexible (invariant from #822). Full grounding in reports/2026-06-17-custom-element-tag-naming-convention.md.

title: Declarative scoped-registration spelling on <component> — how a definition targets a non-global CustomElementRegistry, native-aligned
summary: Prep research for decision #854 (carved from epic #076): how a <component> declares it registers into a scoped CustomElementRegistry instead of the global one. The runtime is already fixed (#228); only the author-facing spelling is open. The item entered prep presuming a scope="<registry-id>" string attribute. The survey of the native proposal RESHAPED the framing and FLIPS that default. Findings: (1) Scoped Custom Element Registries MERGED into the WHATWG DOM/HTML Living Standard 2025-04-14 (whatwg/dom#1341; revamp whatwg/html#10854) — a registry is `new CustomElementRegistry()`, a JS OBJECT with no name/id; bound to a subtree at attachShadow({ mode, customElementRegistry }) (option is customElementRegistry, NOT registry), read via shadowRoot.customElementRegistry, late-bound via registry.initialize(root); no option ⇒ global. Shipped Chrome/Edge 146 + Safari 26; Firefox in progress (bug #1874414, Interop 2026) — 2-of-3 engines, not yet Baseline. (2) A declarative form EXISTS — shadowrootcustomelementregistry on <template shadowrootmode> (Chrome blog; serialization whatwg/html#11892) — but it is PRESENCE-ONLY/boolean: it marks a DSD root's registry as null (awaiting init), and CANNOT name a registry; you bind one from JS afterwards. The platform shipped a declarative form and DELIBERATELY declined to put a registry id in markup — directly contradicting the presumed string scope=. (3) NO library uses a string-id scope: @open-wc/scoped-elements (static scopedElements={tag:Class}), Lit @lit-labs/scoped-registry-mixin (static elementDefinitions), and the @webcomponents polyfill all key on a registry OBJECT reachable via the host class. (4) Native splits TWO concerns the item's scope= fused: CONSUMPTION (which registry markup inside resolves against — has a declarative boolean + attachShadow({customElementRegistry})) vs DEFINITION PLACEMENT (which registry a definition lands in — imperative registry.define() ONLY, no declarative form). <component> is a definition element, so its scope question is the placement concern — exactly the one with no native declarative spelling; a string scope="<id>" would reintroduce a global string namespace (the thing scoping removes), match no library, and not survive a native DSD round-trip. Tree grounding: WE's runtime is already object-keyed (plugs/webregistries/CustomElementRegistry.ts new CustomElementRegistry({extends:[...]}); #228 ensureNativelyConstructible private tags); #242 reserved a RegistryScope token + define(tag,ctor,scope?) for imperative scoped placement (its optional id:string is a tension to reconcile — treat as a WE-only label, the object is the real key); declarativeComponent.ts:162 currently emits a GLOBAL define; component.njk:105/:149 pre-commit to a Tier-2 scope attribute (a presumption to reconcile). RESHAPE: 3 bullets collapse to ONE genuine fork (the author surface); native alignment is the deciding criterion (not a fork); lowering is a consequence (seam-ready via #242). Recommended default REVERSES the title: a presence-only BOOLEAN marker forwarding the native shadowrootcustomelementregistry/customElementRegistry path with the registry OBJECT bound imperatively via the #242 seam — NOT a string attribute (~80%; residual: whether ergonomics justify a human-readable string handle as opt-in sugar layered on top). Full grounding in reports/2026-06-17-854-scope-declarative-scoped-registration-spelling.md.

title: behavior/extends resolution mechanism on <component> — how a registry-key tier-2 hook resolves to a class/trait
summary: Prep research for decision #852 (carved from epic #076; DC-5/#044 ratified the POLICY 2026-06-08 — a tier-2 behavior/extends attribute associates a REGISTERED class/trait, never inline <script>, keeping the XSS-gadget surface closed). DC-5 left the MECHANISM open: how a registry-key resolves to a class/trait and applies to the generated element without breaching the wc-class lowering's invariants (a self-contained ESM with NO import-map seam + a fixed member order for byte-determinism, declarativeComponent.ts:152-208). The item entered prep with ONE fork (resolution time+seam: A build-time inline / B runtime-global / C import, default A ~70%) plus an extends-vs-behavior sub-call; the in-tree CustomAttributeRegistry is the wrong unit (attributes, not element class/trait mixins) so there was no precedent to mirror. The survey splits the fork in two. WICG declarative-custom-elements #1009 (the proposal DC-5 built on) lists the open questions VERBATIM — 'Must the script be inline, or can it be bundled? Can it be loaded late?' (= WE's build-time-vs-runtime axis, unpicked, so WE is genuinely greenfield) and 'base class: extend HTMLElement, or another class with shared behavior?' (names BOTH spellings, validating extends + behavior). Two shipping poles map onto WE's EXISTING twin: Enhance (HTML-first custom elements expanded build/server-side + separate hydration script = option A, build-time inline) and Stimulus (data-controller resolved against a runtime controller registry, custom-resolver name->class = option B, runtime lookup) — these aren't competing winners, they're the two delivery modes WE already ships (generateClassSource built/self-contained form ~ Enhance; defineFromDefinition unbuilt-runtime form ~ Stimulus, no build to inline into). The behavior application shape is a settled idiom: a subclass-factory mixin Behavior(Base) (TC39 proposal-mixins; Lit mixins), extends is the simpler class X extends Base. RESHAPE: Fork 1 (built/static-HTML form) = build-time inline (A, default ~85%) — import seam C is the named-broken branch (breaks the no-import-seam invariant that makes the form copy-pasteable static HTML, declarativeComponent.ts:204-207; the functional twin's import seam is the contrast), B a needless runtime global + ordering hazard. Fork 2 (unbuilt-runtime form's seam, where build-time inlining is IMPOSSIBLE) = reuse the webinjectors DI registry register/consume (default ~70%, Injector.ts:146-152 already does lazy hierarchical resolution + dedup; the running element consults it at definition time, a legitimate runtime-DI seam, not a cargo-cult global) over a new dedicated global registry or an import seam. DISSOLVED (supported by default, not forks): extends AND behavior both ship as two composable emit shapes (extends Base / Behavior(Base)); registry-key-only/never-inline-code is the ratified DC-5 XSS floor. Full grounding in reports/2026-06-17-component-behavior-extends-resolution.md.

title: WE→FUI wrapper-handoff mechanism — how the FUI panel obtains + live-tests WE-generated framework wrappers
summary: Prep research for decision #855 (the cross-repo seam blocking the polyglot adapter panel #753, under Block Explorer epic #746). #821 shipped a WE-owned consume-mode wrapper generator (scripts/gen-wrapper/genWrapper.mjs): a pure generateWrapper(declaration, target) => wrapperSourceString over a block’s Custom Elements Manifest. The panel that DISPLAYS + LIVE-TESTS that source is FUI (locus:frontierui, per the docs-rendering boundary — WE never renders FUI block code). Open seam: how the FUI panel obtains + live-tests the WE-generated wrapper across the repo boundary. Item filed three options cold — (A) a build-time published artifact, (B) a bundle the fuiDemo iframe (#701) loads, (C) a sandbox that generates on demand. Two tree facts shrink it first: the generator is already a pure, importable, no-DOM/no-FUI function the codebase explicitly intends FUI to import directly (#821 cli.mjs note), and consume-mode output is a PURE FUNCTION OF THE CEM (deterministic, no per-user input) so on-demand generation produces output that never varies — the freshness case for (C) evaporates. TOPIC 1 (distribution): the dominant pattern is unambiguous and one-directional — wrappers are a BUILD-TIME ARTIFACT OF THE SOURCE LIBRARY, published as a package the consumer reads; on-demand/runtime generation in the consumer has NO production precedent. Stencil output targets generate in the component library’s build (internal metadata, not CEM) and publish an npm package; Lit Labs gen-wrapper-react/-vue/-angular generate at the element library’s build FROM the custom-elements.json (CEM-driven — the exact shape of #821) and publish; @lit/react createComponent is the lone RUNTIME path but is hand-wired per element, not CEM codegen. This settles A vs C and dissolves B: (A) build-time is the universal precedent; (C) on-demand has no precedent and no benefit for deterministic output; (B) only describes WHERE the bundle sits, an impl detail of the chosen handoff, not a distinct mechanism. TOPIC 2 (live-test sandbox): the render is FUI’s regardless of handoff (embedded via #701 fuiDemo iframe), so the sandbox tech is FUI impl WE does not mandate — guidance only: esm.sh + import maps is the lightest credible multi-framework path (React TSX AND Vue SFC, no COOP/COEP isolation tax inside an embedded iframe); Sandpack is the turnkey source-string-in middle ground; esbuild-wasm fits only a React-only commitment (no Vue SFC); WebContainers is overkill + pays a cross-origin-isolation embedding tax. WHAT SURVIVES: one forced invariant (build-time handoff A, FUI-owned sandbox) + one genuine fork — what crosses the WE→FUI boundary: A1 WE publishes the derived wrapper artifact as a versioned @webeverything/* package (matches Stencil/Lit publish-a-wrapper-package; con: a third published thing that is only a pure function of two already-published WE things) vs A2 WE publishes the generator + CEM contract (@webeverything/gen-wrapper + the ratified CEM #626) and the FUI demo build derives wrappers itself (single source of truth, fresh by construction, matches the #821 authoring intent; con: WE codegen runs inside FUI’s build, less faithful to the Stencil/Lit generate-at-source locus). Recommended default A2 (med-high): the generator already exists as a pure importable function the codebase intends FUI to call; residual = prior-art fidelity (Stencil/Lit generate at the source and publish the wrapper), so A1 is added later if WE wants a sole-generation execution site or a cacheable pre-built artifact — not excluded, just not the probe default. Sequencing (not a fork): blocks.json carries no tagName today so the CEM emits 0 declarations (#822) — the panel build #753 is gated on BOTH this decision and #822; #855 settles the mechanism only. Full grounding in reports/2026-06-17-we-fui-wrapper-handoff.md.

title: Mode-C host→FUI config transport — how a same-realm in-document host hands WE-owned chrome config to a generic FUI module
summary: Prep research for decision #881 (the WE→FUI mode-C host-config transport, gating the WE-docs chrome migration #865, under the dogfooding epic #777). Mode C (#786) mounts a FUI component in WE's own DOM behind a shadow root with NO config channel — the SDK calls mod.mountInDocument(root) with only the ShadowRoot (frontierui/embed/in-document.ts:89, contract frontierui/embed/contract.ts:72-74). The dogfooded chrome (#865) needs WE to supply its nav tree / brand / footer (WE-owned information architecture = content) to FUI's app-shell/sectioned-nav/button blocks (#870), which expose mount*(root, config) factories. The #765 docs-rendering boundary forbids baking WE's IA into a FUI module, so host config must cross WE→FUI at mount time — a reusable cross-repo convention every future mode-C host inherits. LOAD-BEARING GROUNDING WRINKLE the survey resolved: AppShellConfig takes LIVE DOM Nodes (nav?: Node, footer?: Node, headerControls?: Node[] — AppShell.ts:29-43), but mode C has NO host->module JS handoff point (the SDK imports the module and calls mountInDocument(root); the host never gets a factory reference). The only host-reachable handle inside the module is root.host plus whatever the host left in the DOM. So ANY transport riding the existing SDK is inherently DOM-mediated and must carry a SERIALIZABLE projection; the generic FUI module deserializes it and rebuilds the Node tree via the sub-block factories before calling mountAppShell. The decision therefore defines a JSON-serializable ChromeConfig schema, NOT a pass-through of AppShellConfig. The host->component configuration space (browser platform + third-party embed kits) splits FOUR families: (1) data-* attribute JSON on the mount point — the dominant third-party-widget convention (Stripe Elements, Cloudflare Turnstile, analytics embeds), standards-compliant, decoupled, degrades cleanly, but strings need JSON.parse and deep structures inflate via HTML entity-encoding; (2) <script type="application/json"> config sidecar inside the mount point — the canonical platform pattern (JSON-LD, Inertia data-page), same parse step but NO entity-encoding overhead (a measured case dropped 179->98 chars) and reads naturally for a deep nav tree, i.e. the ERGONOMIC REFINEMENT of family 1, not a rival channel; (3) JS properties / typed argument — the web-component-native way to pass complex objects, what a contract change (mountInDocument(root, hostConfig?)) buys, but it needs a JS handoff point mode C lacks, so it means changing the #786 contract + SDK + every consumer; (4) light-DOM declarative HTML the module reads/enhances (the 'HTML web component' pattern) — doubles as the no-JS SSR baseline, but #865 keeps base.njk as that baseline and scopes PE fallback out, so a variant to note not adopt. MAPPED ONTO #881: Fork 1 (channel) families 1+2 = Option A (host writes config as data on the mount point; generic FUI module reads root.host and calls the #870 factories) — the survey REFINES A by adopting the <script type=application/json> sidecar as the carrier for the deep nav tree, dissolving A's only residual (JSON-in-attribute ergonomics); family 3 = Option B (extend the contract), coherent but a contract change for a chrome-only need today, a clean LATER promotion; C (WE-specific FUI-hosted module) stays rejected on the #765 boundary; D (global) stays strictly worse than per-mount-point locality. Fork 2 (granularity) unmoved: app-shell is the composing block and static chrome has no independent-lifecycle need, so one mount point / one config blob (A) holds. Net: the survey CONFIRMS A and dissolves its residual, and adds the serializable-ChromeConfig + script-sidecar constraint the build inherits. Full grounding in reports/2026-06-17-881-mode-c-host-config-transport.md.

title: Monolith-vs-per-file for hand-authored structured specs — how comparable tooling stores per-component/per-project config
summary: Prep research for decision #882 (split the 4657-line src/_data/blocks.json — the per-block structural contract, 75 blocks — into one file per block assembled by a src/_data/blocks.js glob loader). This RATIFIES A SHIPPED INTERNAL PATTERN, not a greenfield standard: the repo already runs glob-and-assemble 12× via _data/*.js loaders, and the canonical proof is the 866-item backlog (one backlog/<NNN-slug>.md per item, NO backlog.json, assembled by src/_data/backlog.js). blocks.json is the lone per-item surface that never followed — and its OWN behavioral half already did (src/_includes/block-descriptions/<id>.njk, 80 files). So the survey is light on browser standards and heavy on (a) the real reader inventory and (b) how comparable ecosystems made the same monolith->per-file call. READER INVENTORY (the assembled `blocks` array is consumed two ways): (1) as the 11ty `blocks` _data global by every template/loader-fed validator that reads the ARRAY (src/blocks.njk, block-pages.njk, index.njk, presets.njk, …) — a blocks.js loader emitting the same array shape leaves all of these UNTOUCHED; (2) by direct file read in standalone scripts that need the glob — gen-cem.mjs:37 (the only hard readFileSync), check-standards.mjs:91, check-app-conformance.mjs:45, gen-reference-index.mjs:34. TWO MIGRATION WRINKLES surfaced by reading the tree (absent from the item's original plan): (i) autofix WRITE-TARGET — check-standards-rules.mjs:572 (FILE.Block='src/_data/blocks.json') and :92 (buildGraduatedKinds block.file) are the descriptor `file` pointers the autofix engine WRITES when patching a block's status; splitting means these must resolve per-block (_data/blocks/<id>.json) so a fixer patches the right small file (net-positive but a required repoint); (ii) graduatedTo ANCHOR GRAMMAR — normalize-graduated.mjs:39 (['block','blocks.json']) + the FILE_ANCHOR regex at :75 define the canonical blocks.json#<id> anchor used across the backlog's graduatedTo fields, a stable cross-backlog contract; after the split blocks.json#<id> must keep resolving as a VIRTUAL anchor (grammar stays, file goes) or every graduatedTo value migrates — keep the virtual anchor. EXTERNAL PRIOR ART, two on-point precedents: (1) NX MIGRATED AWAY FROM THE MONOLITH FOR THIS EXACT REASON — it shipped 15-7-0-split-configuration-into-project-json-files which DELETES the workspace-wide angular.json/workspace.json and replaces it with per-project project.json files, explicitly because the monolith 'can be a source of merge conflicts' for medium/large workspaces and per-file gives 'less possibility of merge conflicts'; the consumed artifact (Nx's resolved project graph) is COMPUTED from the per-file inputs, exactly like the #882 assembled `blocks` array — a major ecosystem made the identical call for #882's identical #1 driver (concurrent-edit conflicts) and chose per-file. (2) STYLE DICTIONARY — takes an ARRAY OF FILE-PATH GLOBS and DEEP-MERGES them into one dictionary ('split the files however you want'); two load-bearing details — file/dir structure has NO effect on the consumed object (layout decoupled from assembled shape, validates the loader), and the merge is ORDER-DEPENDENT ('later files take precedence') so the glob loader MUST impose a DETERMINISTIC SORT, not trust readdirSync order. Adjacent: Storybook CSF = one story file per component; custom-elements-manifest aggregates to one custom-elements.json per package but only because it is GENERATED, not hand-authored (aggregation OK for a build artifact, not for a hand-edited source of truth like blocks.json). DID THE SURVEY RESHAPE THE FORKS? No fork added/dissolved (direction was strongly internally precedented), but it (a) HARDENED the default via an external precedent that made the identical call for the identical reason (Nx, raising Fork-1 confidence ~85%->~88%), (b) added a DETERMINISM requirement to the loader (Style Dictionary merge-order caveat), (c) surfaced the two migration constraints above. HANDED TO #882: Fork 1 (granularity) A=per-file (default, ~88%); Fork 2 (layout) B1=flat src/_data/blocks/<id>.json (default ~70%, reversible toward B2 co-located bundle later). Classification: webdocs build/authoring-surface concern, mints NO protocol/intent (#657 pinned the field set = the contract; #882 is purely file granularity), fixed mechanic not a per-block dimension, separation bias favors per-file. Full grounding in reports/2026-06-17-blocks-json-per-file-split.md.

title: Plateau-creator → FUI-switcher manifest transport — how a cross-origin, runtime-authored #747 manifest reaches the FUI-resident #749 switcher
summary: Prep research for decision #887 (the Plateau-creator → FUI-switcher manifest transport, gating the #751 'Your theme' embed capstone, under the Block Explorer epic #746). The #751 capstone wants a 'Your theme' button on the FUI block workbench that opens the embedded Plateau design-system creator (an iframe — Plateau owns the creator, FUI/WE embed it) and feeds the author's freshly-authored #747 manifest to the live #749 switcher so the created theme applies to the real block immediately. The creator (Plateau, cross-origin) and the switcher (FUI, frontierui/workbench/mount.ts) live in DIFFERENT cross-origin iframes. So how does a RUNTIME-AUTHORED manifest cross the Plateau→FUI seam, and should the FUI switcher consume an externally-supplied manifest at all vs only its fixed FUI-resident gallery? This is the explicit CROSS-ORIGIN, RUNTIME-AUTHORED COUSIN of #881. #881 ruled the same-origin, static case (WE hands server-emitted nav/brand/footer to a FUI mode-C mount in WE's own DOM) and landed on DOM-mediated data on the mount point — because same-origin DOM is readable and the config is build-time. #887 flips BOTH distinguishing properties: cross-origin (Plateau iframe ↔ FUI iframe) and runtime (user-authored in the creator) — so the same-origin policy blocks all cross-frame DOM/contentDocument reads, and a cooperating message channel is the only carrier. The rhyme resolves OPPOSITELY on the channel: #881 → DOM-data (same-origin + static); #887 → postMessage (cross-origin + runtime). GROUNDING THE REAL TREES: (1) An origin-validated postMessage channel ALREADY EXISTS in the FUI embed SDK — embed-host.ts installs a message router that rejects any message whose event.origin ≠ the frame's validated origin (embed-host.ts:142), matches event.source to a live contentWindow, and sends host→guest via postToGuest targeted at the validated origin; embed-guest.ts posts to its embedder's origin and origin-gates inbound; the wire contract (contract.ts) is a versioned EmbedEnvelope (channel:'fui-embed', v:1), a GuestMessage|HostMessage union, an isEmbedMessage guard, and an envelope() helper — already an extensible origin-validated message bus (ready/resize/overlay-open/overlay-close/init), so adding an apply-design-system message type is a contract ADDITION, not a new channel. #887-A is NOT greenfield. (2) The switcher's apply path is one already-factored function — frontierui/workbench/mount.ts:277 applyDesignSystem(preset) sets themeTokens as custom properties, sets data-intent-*/data-trait-*, re-renders the same block with no code change; the gallery wires each chip to it. Consuming an external manifest = build a DesignSystemPreset from the payload and call the EXISTING applyDesignSystem(). (3) The transported manifest must be the RESOLVED/INLINE #747 form, not a DTCG-path manifest — the workbench's presets carry themeTokens resolved inline as a {'--custom-prop': value} map ('because the workbench has no WE↔FUI channel … it consumes the #747 manifest shape with its own preset data'); the WE catalog form (designSystems.json) uses manifest path refs, and cross-origin the FUI switcher CANNOT dereference a themeTokens path on a Plateau/WE origin (no channel + CORS), so the creator must serialize the FULLY-RESOLVED manifest (token values inlined) — #887's analogue of #881's serializable-ChromeConfig constraint. TOPOLOGY constrained by #809 (no WE↔FUI channel): the FUI workbench hosts the Plateau creator as a NESTED CHILD iframe (the sanctioned #746 'embedded Plateau configurators' pattern), making creator→switcher a DIRECT Plateau↔FUI parent-child message; the sibling-iframe alternative (WE page hosts both, relays between them) reconstitutes exactly the WE↔FUI channel #809 forbids — EXCLUDED, not weighed. PRIOR-ART FAMILIES for a cross-iframe runtime handoff: (1) postMessage (runtime push, ephemeral) — the platform-standard cross-origin channel and dominant embedded-editor→live-preview pattern (Stripe Elements, Figma/CodeSandbox/StackBlitz embeds, Storybook manager↔preview), always origin-validated, serializable payload, no backend/persistence/reload → the live-apply channel; (2) persist-to-backend + fetch-by-id (durable) — the share/persist pattern (Tokens Studio Pro sync, zeroheight/Supernova), and per #775 the PAID tier (hosted persistence), right for save-and-share but wrong as the live-apply channel (drags a free zero-friction loop behind hosted infra + a round-trip); (3) URL/navigation handoff (encode + reload) — WE already has the serialization in #754, but #754 encodes a manifest REF not inline tokens and a reload-per-tweak destroys live authoring. MAPPED ONTO #887: Fork 1 (live-apply transport) — family 1 = Option A (origin-validated postMessage over the existing FUI embed channel + an apply-design-system message + the Plateau creator's trusted origin), family 2 = Option B (persist+fetch — the paid share path, a structural defect as the live channel), family 3 = Option C (URL/#754 reload — share/deep-link, breaks live iteration) → A holds, strengthened by the existing origin-validated channel being directly reusable. Forced invariants the survey hardened (not forks): Plateau→FUI direct + WE never brokers (#809); the switcher consumes the runtime manifest (fixed-gallery-only is the broken branch — severs #751's loop; injection point is the existing applyDesignSystem() at mount.ts:277); the wire shape is the resolved/inline manifest (token values inlined). Full grounding in reports/2026-06-17-887-plateau-creator-fui-switcher-transport.md.

title: Home for a generic WE-conformance / schema-validation service — vectors (WE data) vs the runnable validator (Plateau open-core vs independent)
summary: Prep research for decision #895 (surfaced by #779). Implementers building WE-compatible libraries will need a generic service to validate their schema/manifest against the WE contract. The artifact forks by risk: the conformance VECTORS (declarative input->expected-behavior data) are a WE artifact (data, can't 'fail to deliver'); the runnable VALIDATOR that loads them and checks a library is NOT WE (a buggy runnable tool reads as 'WE is broken' — ownership carries the reputation, npm scope doesn't fix it) and NOT FUI (generic across implementers, FUI is one). Survey of real conformance tooling found a two-layer split nobody violates: the body owns the vectors/suite as neutral data in its own repo (test262, JSON-Schema test suite, WPT), and the runnable validator is decoupled from the body, almost always OSS. Where a runnable tool ships, three shapes: (i) independent/community OSS (JSON-Schema/Ajv, OpenAPI validators — strongest decoupling but presumes a community materializes to build+maintain a runner); (ii) vendor OPEN-CORE = complete open CLI floor + optional hosted SaaS (Spectral/Stoplight, axe-core/Deque — guarantees a runner exists, keeps the firewall iff the OSS floor is a complete standalone conformance check and vectors stay in the body's repo); (iii) standards-body-HOSTED (Nu Html Checker / validator.nu — re-attaches validator bugs to the body, safe only for a mature single-canonical-artifact spec; the model to AVOID for a young standard, restating the user's 'not WE' concern from the survey side). WHAT SURVIVES: forced invariants (vectors->WE @webeverything data pkg; validator not-WE-hosted + not-FUI; open CLI must be a complete standalone floor — the Spectral/axe-core test) + ONE genuine fork: validator home = Plateau-suite open-core (free CLI floor + optional hosted Plateau service) vs independent open devtool repo. Recommended default Plateau open-core ~75%: pure independent OSS is purest decoupling but a solo-dev pre-community standard won't get a community runner (vectors left with no runner); open-core guarantees a runner without re-coupling reputation, aligns with constellation layering (#091 served-product->plateau-app, #475 impl capability=Plateau service) + open-core monetization (#089-#093,#398) with the free/paid line on the cost/hosting axis (free local CLI vs hosted), never open-vs-proprietary. Residual ~25%: single-constellation perception risk — if Plateau reads as de-facto WE owner a validator bug can still splash onto WE despite the formal split; mitigation = the two invariants applied structurally (permissive license, self-hostable, forkable; vectors in WE repo). Independent neutral-governance repo is the correct override IF a genuine multi-implementer community forms. Full grounding in reports/2026-06-18-conformance-validator-home.md.

title: Plateau-creator manifest → FUI workbench-stage token bridge — mapping an open DTCG token set onto the stage's 5 fixed --wb-* render props
summary: Prep research for decision #930 (carved from the #751 'Your theme' embed capstone under Block Explorer #746). #887 ratified the TRANSPORT (origin-validated postMessage carrying the resolved/inline #747 manifest) but explicitly left the apply-design-system message's FIELD LIST / FIDELITY a build detail — and that detail is a genuine SEMANTIC-ALIGNMENT fork: the Plateau creator authors an OPEN DTCG token set while the FUI workbench stage renders through 5 FIXED --wb-* custom properties. THE TWO VOCABULARIES (verified 2026-06-18): Producer — the creator's #747 manifest (plateau-app/src/design-system-creator/manifest.ts:18) carries an open, flat themeTokens map ('In WE this is a DTCG ref; the creator authors the values inline'), and the #889 import adapter (importAdapter.ts) ingests ARBITRARY DTCG trees + Figma variable exports, so themeTokens is unbounded; plus intentDefaults keyed by surface/density/motion/typography/theme-color and a free-form traitDefaults slot. Consumer — the FUI stage block reads exactly five custom properties (frontierui/workbench/designSystems.ts DesignSystemTokens: --wb-accent/--wb-radius/--wb-pad/--wb-font/--wb-shadow), applied by applyDesignSystem(preset) at frontierui/workbench/mount.ts:277. The gap is NOT a 1:1 rename: open→finite forbids a pass-through, and the intent/trait enums DON'T EVEN MATCH (creator surface: flat|raised|sunken vs FUI surface: lift|solid; creator radius trait sharp|soft|round vs FUI lg|sm|none) so a pure rename is impossible too — SOME mapping/selection layer is structurally REQUIRED; only where it lives and which vocabulary the block ultimately reads are open. PRIOR ART (the design-token ecosystem converged on a tiered model + an explicit transform/filter step at every render boundary — this is a token-SHAPE question, not the cross-frame transport of #887): (1) tiered tokens primitive→semantic→component (Material 3 reference→system→component; the near-universal DTCG 3-tier convention) — the 5 --wb-* props ARE the demo's component-token tier, DTCG themeTokens are its primitive/semantic tiers, so a cross-tier alias step is the norm not a defect; (2) transform + filter pipeline (Style Dictionary transforms/filters re-use one format with a filtered token SUBSET, SD-Transforms, Theo) — selecting the handful of DTCG tokens a finite stage can express IS the standard filter step, and per-platform lossiness is expected; (3) semantic remap for theming (Material 3 semantic remapping for light/dark/dynamic without touching component logic) — a stable --wb-* slot set whose SOURCES are remapped is how a curated demo stays legible across swapped systems. Every mature system places a semantic→component mapping between an open source and the finite slots a target consumes and treats lossiness there as routine = option A; none pushes the component-token vocabulary UP into the authoring tool (option C — collapses the tiered model + breaks the #889 open import), and none makes a curated demo read the raw open set without a selecting transform (option B still needs the filter — 'consume DTCG directly' mostly RENAMES the slots). CLASSIFICATION (7-question pass): the bridge maps the #747 manifest CONTRACT (a WE standard, already ratified by #871/#887) into FUI's PRIVATE --wb-* render props = pure FUI IMPLEMENTATION (impl-is-not-a-standard; generator-is-a-tool-not-a-WE-standard) — the contract is fixed, only FUI's consumption is in question; it's a fixed mechanic of the curated demo (a data-driven declared binding layer was considered + rejected as YAGNI for 5 props); the producer stays maximally permissive and the RECEIVER absorbs the narrowing (most-permissive-default → A; C inverts it). THE ONE GENUINE FORK — the bridge shape, options A/B/C: A (recommended) a DTCG→--wb-* mapping/transform in the FUI receiver that reads the WHOLE resolved manifest (themeTokens + intentDefaults + traitDefaults), selects/aliases the well-known sources into the 5 slots with declared fallbacks, builds a DesignSystemPreset, calls the existing applyDesignSystem() — lossy by design (the standard filter step), keeps the producer open, keeps the curated demo legible, --wb-* never crosses the WE boundary (no lock-in); a MERIT win not a cost win (a mapping is unavoidable open→finite, and the 5 curated slots are the point of the demo, so B's faithfulness is largely illusory for materially more work). B the stage consumes DTCG directly (rewire block CSS + every preset to DTCG-canonical names) — larger, the block still has finite hooks so a selecting transform is STILL needed, right ONLY IF the stage becomes a production theming engine (a deferred 'stage matures' revisit). C constrain the creator to the --wb-* shape — EXCLUDED on merit (forces the author to emit FUI's private render props, breaks the #889 DTCG/Figma import, inverts the dependency). RECOMMENDATION A ~80%; residual ~20% is the mapping table's exact entries (the --wb-font/--wb-shadow sources + enum-value reconciliation) — LESS lossy than #751's claim assumed since intent/trait defaults give every slot a source (--wb-pad ← density, --wb-shadow ← surface, --wb-radius ← radius trait, --wb-font ← a type.* family token) with the extends base as fallback; that table is a build detail of A, not a separate fork. RED-TEAM for the deciding turn: the attack is 'lossy mapping silently drops tokens — B is faithful'; rebuttal — the block has a finite hook count regardless so B needs the same selecting filter (Style Dictionary's model), its faithfulness is renaming not coverage, and B is the correct end-state only if the stage becomes a production theming runtime (a curated showcase today). Full grounding in reports/2026-06-18-930-creator-manifest-wb-token-bridge.md.

title: Enforcing "compose existing traits over hand-rolled re-implementation" — the conformance-gate mechanism (advisory vs mechanical, detection teeth, declaration home)
summary: Prep research for decision #933. The principle is settled (compose a registered WE trait/behavior for any capability a standard already covers; never hand-roll it with raw addEventListener); the open call is the ENFORCEMENT MECHANISM. Two FUI chrome blocks re-implemented the W3C APG Disclosure Navigation pattern that already exists as the nav:section/nav:list traits — sectioned-nav (#870), then disclosure-nav (#931) — and nothing mechanical caught either. Survey of conformance/lint/fitness-function prior art (WAI-ARIA APG disclosure pattern; ESLint no-restricted-syntax / jsx-a11y; ArchUnit/dependency-cruiser/ts-arch fitness functions; axe-core rendered-vs-static) reshaped the original A/B/C fork: A (advisory pre-flight only) is the EXCLUDED branch (it is what already existed and failed twice → a mechanical gate is a forced invariant, the checklist stays as a complement); C (declarative-only by construction) is NOT a rival but is sequenced after the mode-C registry-boot decision #932/#934 (removes the temptation at source over time); B (mechanical gate) is forced and carries the two genuine open calls. Fork 1 — the gate detection teeth: declaration-resolution + a curated pattern→trait deny-list (disclosure→nav:section, roving-tabindex→nav:list), warn-first per the #840/#844 precedent (default ~75%); reject open-ended addEventListener sniffing (ESLint lesson: open heuristics are false-positive factories that get disabled) and reject declaration-only (misses a block that declares nothing and hand-rolls — the exact #931/#870 mode). Fork 2 — the declaration home: a new explicit block-protocol field for COMPOSED (consumed) traits, projected into CEM x-webeverything and asserted to resolve against traits.json (default ~80%); reject overloading the existing traits field (it means traits a block PROVIDES) and reject the name composesTraits (already rejected 2026-06-06 for The Map materialization, traits.json:29 — name-collision footgun). Key non-obvious finding: axe passed on #931 (a hand-rolled disclosure can be a11y-clean), so a RENDERED conformance check cannot catch this — the defect is visible only in source+declaration, pinning the gate to the static check:standards path beside validateBlockImplConformance (check-standards-rules.mjs:1234). Full grounding in reports/2026-06-18-933-enforce-compose-traits-over-hand-roll.md.

title: Gate-violation attribution — letting a concurrent session's in-flight work not red yours in a shared working tree
summary: Prep research for decision #949: how should the backlog gate (check:standards primarily, check:health secondarily) attribute violations to their owning work so that 'gate red' only stops the session whose change caused it? THE PROBLEM: N agent sessions share ONE working tree; each claims its own items and commits per-finished-item (often straight to main), so the tree holds HEAD (clean) + the UNION of every live session's uncommitted edits. check:standards is whole-repo and exits 1 on any error (check-standards.mjs:930); each finding is {message, descriptor?} and the descriptor carries only a file path (location:{path}, :903), never a backlog-item id or session — so session B's in-flight file reds session A. The standing rule (memory feedback_gate_red_stop_scoped_to_own_work) says step over a purely-external red, but the triage is MANUAL (git status + grep). check:health is the inverse: findings are ALREADY item-keyed (flags G1..G6/D1..D3 each {id:item.id}, audit-backlog-health.mjs:278/:295) and it already shells to git (execFileSync :52/:225/:232) — so health attribution is trivial (filter by claimed item ids); the hard half is file-keyed check:standards. No file->item map exists (frontmatter.mjs:128 tracks graduatedTo/blockedBy, no touches/files); the only per-session state is reservations.json (soft item-number holds keyed by session, TTL-pruned) which neither gate consults. PRIOR ART SURVEYED: (1) Nx affected — git changed-files vs a base ref (default main) -> project graph -> affected subset; base/head explicit, --files override; assumes single-actor/clean-base. (2) reviewdog -filter-mode = added(default)/diff_context/file/nofilter — linter scans everything, findings FILTERED to the diff before failing; 'file' mode (whole file for any changed file) is the granularity #949 wants. (3) Android Lint baseline / betterer — snapshot current violations, fail only on NEW; a TEMPORAL ratchet (old-vs-new in time, globally), not by-owner. (4) lint-staged — lint only git-staged paths. (5) CODEOWNERS — declared path->owner map (the manifest family; declared not derived -> drifts). (6) git worktrees/per-branch isolation — give each actor a clean base so naive whole-repo gating already sees only its work. THE LOAD-BEARING MISMATCH: all changed-files tooling assumes ONE actor against a clean base ref, so git diff vs HEAD IS 'my change'; our problem is N actors sharing ONE dirty tree with no per-actor branch, where git diff vs HEAD = the UNION (the over-attribution we're undoing). Recovering 'mine' needs extra per-session state single-actor tooling never carries — OR the structural worktree fix. This reshaped the fork: the survey surfaced worktree-isolation, an option the item's 'make the gate attribute' framing didn't name -> it becomes the top-level fork. SYNTHESIS handed to #949 — three forks. Fork 1 (strategy): in-gate attribution vs worktree-per-session isolation; recommend in-gate (~80%, preserves shared-tree + running-dev-server + commit-to-main workflow; isolation is the heavier escalation, though Agent already supports isolation:worktree). Fork 2 (signal): per-session git baseline snapshot at claim (derived not declared, fails safe on the in-window leak) vs declared frontmatter manifest (drifts; against derive-from-state) vs session file-claim registry extending reservations.json (most precise, adds a hook + orphan cleanup); recommend the baseline snapshot (~75%). Fork 3 (behavior): a --scope filter mode (exit 1 only on in-scope errors, default no-flag run unchanged) vs a baseline ratchet (wrong axis — temporal not by-owner, + shared mutable baseline races) vs annotate-only (doesn't change the exit, fails the ask); recommend --scope (~85%). Supported by default (not forks): check:health reuses the scope mode keyed on item-id directly; scope granularity is the SESSION's whole claimed set. Classification: repo-internal devtools/workflow tooling, not a WE standard (impl-is-not-a-standard, minimize-lock-in) — no protocol minted; a correctness gate's safe default is STRICT, scoping is an opt-in per-run flag (dimension, not baked). Full grounding in reports/2026-06-18-gate-violation-attribution.md.

title: Slate editor engine — may a React-coupled library engine live in FUI's opt-in engine set, and how?
summary: Prep research for decision #963 (slice of the rich-text-editor engine-adapters epic #940, behind the CustomEditorEngine seam #923 shipped at fui:blocks/rich-text-editor/editorEngine.ts:34 — attach(ctx)->handle, HTML pivot). #940 registers four real library engines; three (Quill/Lexical/ProseMirror) are clean parallel slices, Slate is held out because its editing surface slate-react is React-based, so a Slate engine pulls React into FUI even as an optional dep (same concern as #955 Fork B, the polyglot live-test sandbox). SURVEYED the four libraries' real architectures: Slate is the ONLY one whose view layer mandates a framework — Quill is vanilla (Parchment/Delta), ProseMirror is vanilla (prosemirror-view EditorView, powers Tiptap/Remirror/BlockNote), Lexical is framework-agnostic with @lexical/headless (DOM-less) and @lexical/react as ONE optional binding; Slate's `slate` core is framework-agnostic but slate-react is the only mature view (the docs split the core so 'other view layers like React Native or Vue.js' are POSSIBLE in future — not shipped; no official non-React view). The non-React Slate views that exist (svelte-slate by nathanfaucett, slate-vue) are community, pull a DIFFERENT framework (Svelte/Vue), and lag core — so 'non-React render' never means no-framework, it means a different less-maintained one. CONSEQUENCE for option A (headless core + custom non-React render): the seam already ships NativeContentEditableEngine (hand-written contenteditable view); 'Slate core + custom FUI view' = re-writing slate-react's view layer (selection sync, IME, decorations) — the hardest part of Slate and exactly what makes Slate Slate. The product would be 'the native engine but storing state in Slate's JSON model' — it discards the reason anyone picks Slate, so A doesn't DELIVER a Slate engine (fails the goal, not just costlier; the Svelte/Vue-view variant is strictly worse than React on every axis). A is the flawed branch — dissolved. With A gone the call reduces to ONE axis: the scope of FUI's framework-free principle. B (ship Slate via slate-react, React optional peerDep) vs C (drop Slate) — and C is principled only if the rule forbids React in ANY @frontierui artifact; else 'drop Slate' is a prioritization call (defer the build), not a fork branch (not-a-prioritization rule). Floor-only reading (recommended): the #940 epic's own words — 'libraries stay peer/optional deps so the native FLOOR is dependency-free'; #955 protects FUI's default/shipped bundle ('React/Vue load only inside the dev-tool sandbox, never in FUI's shipped bundle'); an opt-in, tree-shakeable engine=slate adapter bringing its own React peerDep is fine (libraries are opt-in enhancements, most-permissive default); #955's broken branch B2 was BUNDLING React into FUI's core for every consumer — materially different from an engine that tree-shakes away unless explicitly imported. No-React-anywhere reading: any React in a published @frontierui artifact dilutes the framework-free identity + adds a peer-dep matrix (React 18/19, ReactDOM, slate-react coupling) -> Slate excluded. Lexical (modern nested-JSON, React-ecosystem origin, genuinely headless core) already covers Slate's niche WITHOUT the React mandate, making Slate's marginal coverage small — but that's a prioritization input (build-now?), not an end-state exclusion. SYNTHESIS handed to #963: one genuine fork = the scope of the framework-free principle, outcomes B (permit opt-in React engine) vs C (forbid->drop); recommended default B/floor-only ~70% (med-high); residual = the stricter no-React-in-@frontierui identity reading the decider (owns the framework-free stance) may pull at ratify — the red-team attack to brief. Classification: engine adapters are FUI impl behind the WE editor-engine protocol #629 (no WE standard artifact changes); engine set is a swappable dimension not a fixed mechanic; opt-in Slate is the permissive default. Full grounding in reports/2026-06-18-slate-engine-react-coupling.md.

title: Polyglot live-test sandbox — executing a generated framework wrapper live in the block workbench (isolation + runtime source)
summary: Prep research for decision #955 (blocks #912; Block Explorer epic #746, locus frontierui): how to EXECUTE (not just show) the React/Vue wrapper genWrapper emits and render it live with error surfacing. Tracing the real output (fui:tools/gen-wrapper/genWrapper.mjs:119) the React wrapper is literally `import React … return React.createElement('<fui-tag>', {ref,…}, children)` — so a live preview needs three things absent from the workbench today: (1) in-browser TS+JSX transpile, (2) a React/Vue runtime reachable from the preview, (3) the real custom element DEFINED where the wrapper runs. The workbench registers blocks via bundler-resolved dynamic imports (fui:workbench/registry.ts:149 `import('../blocks/droplist/AutoComplete')`) exposing NO stable per-block module URL to re-import elsewhere. The transpiler (esbuild-wasm vs Babel-standalone) is a confirmed impl detail; the two real axes are element isolation + framework-runtime source.
